does: This practitioner's reference provides a comprehensive treatment of the full technical stack required to build autonomous AI systems, from foundational LLM components through deployment. It covers the LLM substrate including transformer architecture, training techniques like SFT and LoRA, and inference optimization; the alignment and reasoning layer encompassing RLHF, PPO, DPO variants, and test-time scaling methods; and agentic systems including trajectory-based reinforcement learning, retrieval-augmented generation, memory architectures spanning in-context to semantic storage, and agent harness design patterns. The central argument is that building effective agentic systems demands integrated understanding across all pipeline layers rather than isolated expertise in single components.
connects: This work synthesizes decades of developments in deep learning infrastructure and recent advances in large language models with emerging techniques for agentic behavior. It builds on foundational transformer research and GPU-scale training methods, extends classical reinforcement learning theory through modern variants like DPO and GRPO that align language models with human preferences, and incorporates recent agentic approaches including in-context learning and retrieval augmentation that emerged from scaling studies. The book responds to the practical gap between theoretical AI capabilities and deployed systems by organizing knowledge across the traditional boundaries between infrastructure, alignment, and decision-making subsystems that prior work often treated separately.
enables: This comprehensive treatment enables practitioners to architect production agentic systems with principled understanding of tradeoffs across components rather than treating modules as black boxes. It provides a foundation for future research bridging agentic training at scale, more sophisticated memory and retrieval systems, and next-generation agent designs that integrate multiple reasoning paradigms. The systematic stack presentation enables clearer identification of bottlenecks in current agentic systems and guides development of hybrid approaches that exploit synergies between classical planning, modern learned components, and retrieval mechanisms for increasingly complex autonomous reasoning tasks.
